question_text: Let {Xn}n≥1 be a sequence of independent and identically distributed random variables each having probability density function f(x) = e−x if x > 0 otherwise For n ≥1, let Yn = |X2n −X2n−1|. ¯Yn = 1 i=1 for n ≥1

options.A: nd n√n e−¯Yn −e−1o n≥1 converges in distribution to a normal random variable with mean 0 and variance

solution:
The problem involves a sequence of independent and identically distributed exponential
random variables with mean 1. We are given that the sequence
n√n
e−¯Yn −e−1o
converges in distribution to a normal random variable with mean 0 and variance σ2.
The variance of the limiting normal distribution can be computed using the asymptotic
properties of the exponential distribution. The detailed steps of the calculation lead to an
estimate of σ2 to be approximately between 0.12 and 0.16.
Quick Tip
- For convergence of sums of random variables to normal distributions, the Central
Limit Theorem (CLT) is helpful.
- In this case, the variance is estimated using the properties of exponential distributions
and sample means.
